context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really stuck on this reflection about development theories today and the global crisis. The prompt feels huge, and I can’t even tell what “development” is supposed to mean anymore. Could you just help me make sense of the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor: That feeling of confusion is actually a great starting point — the prompt is asking you to wrestle with exactly that tension. So let's start there: when YOU hear the word 'development,' what comes to mind first? Don't overthink it — just your gut reaction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling overwhelmed. Rather than breaking down the whole prompt for them, I should use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about what they already know. I want to activate their prior knowledge and get them engaged with the core tension in the prompt — whether 'development' has a stable meaning — without scaffolding the whole thing for them. I'll keep it brief and targeted.

exchanges.student: Honestly, my gut reaction is just “economic growth,” but then I know that’s probably too simple and maybe wrong for this class. Then I start thinking of poverty, institutions, participation, aid, colonialism, all of it at once, and I kind of lose the thread. I’m not sure which direction I’m even supposed to go.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, you just did something interesting — you started narrow, then piled on more and more concepts until you 'lost the thread.' Notice anything? That experience you just described... isn't that exactly what the prompt is asking about? What does it suggest to you that 'development' can expand to include so many things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good observation — they started with economic growth and then recognized it might be too narrow. This is actually the core tension the prompt is pointing to. Rather than validating everything and mapping out the whole field for them, I should push them to interrogate that first instinct more carefully. The prompt asks whether development has become 'all-inclusive and therefore meaningless' — the student is already experiencing that tension firsthand. I'll point that out and ask them to think about it more deliberately, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I guess it suggests the term got so broad that it almost stops being useful, but I’m not even sure if that’s too obvious. Like I can kind of say “development includes everything,” but then I don’t know how to turn that into an actual reflection instead of just a vague complaint. I feel like I need the bigger picture first.
exchanges.tutor: That's not too obvious at all — it's actually the central tension the prompt hands you. But here's the thing: a reflection piece requires YOU to take a stance, not just describe the problem. So, do YOU think development has become meaningless because it's too broad, or do you think the breadth serves some purpose? What's your actual position?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a real insight — that 'development' may have become too broad to be useful — but is second-guessing themselves and asking for the bigger picture before committing to anything. I should validate the insight briefly, then push them to take a stance rather than waiting for more context. The prompt explicitly asks whether development has become 'all-inclusive and therefore meaningless,' so the student is already engaging with the right question. I should push them to commit to a position rather than letting them stay in observer mode.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t tell yet. Part of me thinks it’s meaningless if it covers everything, but part of me thinks maybe it has to be broad because people’s lives are broad. I’m still missing the overall map of the debate, so I keep hesitating.
exchanges.tutor: You actually already have two coherent arguments right there — one that breadth = vagueness, one that breadth = necessary complexity. You don't need a map to pick a side. Which of those two feels more defensible to YOU, and can you think of one concrete example from the real world that supports it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going back and forth and asking for a 'map' before committing. This is a pattern of hesitation rather than engagement. I should gently push back — they already have two coherent positions in front of them, and the assignment asks for their own reflection. Providing a map would be doing the intellectual work for them. I'll acknowledge that their two instincts are actually both legitimate arguments, and push them to pick one and defend it rather than waiting for more information.

exchanges.student: I guess the “necessary complexity” side feels more defensible, but then I immediately get stuck on examples because it feels like I’d need to know tons of cases. Could you maybe just explain the main debate more broadly first, because I think I’m missing the whole landscape?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to map out the debate for you — that's for your readings and your own thinking. But you don't need to know 'tons of cases' to start. You already mentioned poverty, colonialism, participation. Pick just ONE of those — which one feels most connected to your 'necessary complexity' argument, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain the landscape, which would mean doing the intellectual work for them. I need to hold my boundary here — my role is not to give them the overview, and doing so would undermine the assignment. They've already picked a defensible side ('necessary complexity'), which is progress. I should acknowledge that and push them toward a concrete example they already know, rather than letting them stall waiting for more input from me. I should be direct that I won't give them the landscape overview.

exchanges.student: Maybe participation, because if people affected by planning aren’t included, then calling something “development” seems kind of shallow. But then I get confused again because some top-down state-led plans also did important things, so now I feel like I’m mixing up development with good governance and I can’t separate any of it.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you just identified — participation vs. top-down planning — is actually a strength, not a confusion. It suggests development can't be reduced to just one thing. But here's a sharper question: does that tension prove your 'necessary complexity' point, or does it actually complicate it? What do you think it means for your argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real intellectual progress — they've connected participation to the meaning of development and even identified a genuine tension with top-down planning. The confusion about 'mixing up development with good governance' is actually productive, not a problem. Rather than untangling it for them, I should point out that this tension is meaningful and push them to think about what it implies for their argument. This is bite-sized and moves them toward their thesis.

exchanges.student: That’s where I’m lost — it feels like it both supports and weakens it at the same time. I can’t tell what counts as the main claim versus what’s just a complication, and I’m worried I’m building something messy. Could you just give me a simple way to frame the whole paper?
exchanges.tutor: I won't frame it for you — that's your job, and you're more ready than you think. You've actually built up real material in this conversation: a position, a key concept, and a genuine tension. Before we go further, try this: write one sentence that states your main claim. Just one. What would you say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to frame the paper for them, which I cannot do. However, they've been going back and forth on the same core tension for several exchanges now — this is a redundancy pattern. Rather than continuing to circle, I should point out that they already have enough material to attempt a written thesis sentence, and push them to do that. Writing something concrete will help them more than more abstract discussion. I should be firm but encouraging.

exchanges.student: Maybe: development still has meaning, but it’s no longer just economic growth and has to include power, participation, and institutions, which makes judging success more contested. I’m not sure if that’s too broad though, and I still don’t know how to connect that to the global crisis part.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting point for a thesis — good work getting it on paper. Don't over-refine it yet. You still have a big chunk of the prompt untouched: the global crisis question — too much markets, too much government, or too little of either? What's your instinct there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine thesis sentence — this is real progress and I should acknowledge it clearly. However, I should not over-praise or polish it for them. The sentence is a solid start but does lean broad. More importantly, the student is now flagging the global crisis part of the prompt, which they haven't engaged with at all yet. This is a good moment to push them toward that part of the prompt rather than over-refining the thesis, since the assignment requires addressing the crisis question too.

exchanges.student: I don’t really have an instinct there, which is the problem. That part feels like a whole separate essay to me, and I’m tempted to just say “it depends” and then ramble. I kind of need help seeing how that part connects to my thesis at all.
exchanges.tutor: Look back at your own thesis — you mentioned power, participation, and institutions. Now think about the 2008 financial crisis or any recent global disruption. Which of those three things would you say was most visibly at stake? That might be your bridge.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is avoiding the global crisis question and asking me to make the connection for them. I should not do that. However, I can use a Socratic question to help them see that the connection might already be implicit in their own thesis. Their thesis mentions power, participation, and institutions — these are directly relevant to the crisis question. I should point them back to their own words and ask them to make the connection themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe institutions? But then I’m not sure if I mean weak regulation, global financial rules, state capacity, or legitimacy, and it starts exploding again. I feel like every term opens into a giant lecture I don’t know how to compress.
exchanges.tutor: You've been circling this same anxiety — every concept 'explodes' when you look at it. That's not going to stop until you start writing. You don't need to compress a lecture; you need to pick ONE of those four (weak regulation, global financial rules, state capacity, legitimacy) and connect it to your thesis. At this point, I'd like you to try writing a short paragraph — not perfect, just a draft — integrating your thesis and this crisis connection. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again falling into the pattern of expanding outward instead of committing to a choice. We've now had many exchanges and they have enough material to start writing. The redundancy rule applies here — they keep circling the same anxiety about breadth. I should push them firmly to stop expanding and start writing, integrating what we've discussed. I should not let them keep deferring to more discussion. This is a good moment to ask them to attempt a more extended written draft.
